posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: More interested in sites than producers, and more interested in sites from which I might some day actually own a bottle (i.e. not La Tache, Romanee-Conti, or La Romanee—though by no means hold back, if you have pertinent observations). On the heels of Jadot thread the other day, I realized I never had a Musigny that I thought fully ready; my suspicion is they take a 40-50 years minimum to really show their stuff. Chambertin is another, but I’ve had older of these, so less noticeable (and certainly “satellite” Chambertin GC’s, Chapelle, Charmes, Griottes, seem drinkable earlier, though maybe not Clos de Beze?). I know folks also say Corton, but these, again, certainly the satellites, seem to come around earlier. Other candidates for needs most time? Related observations?

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I’m far from an expert on red Burgundy, much less Grand Crus. However IMO anything from Burgundy held over 30 years, regardless of provenance, becomes a risk and very, very subject to bottle variation.

You say that Corton comes around earlier. If your time frame is 40 - 50 years minimum (!), I guess you are right. However I am starting to find that good Cortons can open and start to sing in the 20+ range, I haven’t gotten to 30 yet.

Dan Kravitz
From what I’ve read, and based on limited first-hand tasting experience, Bonnes-Mares is the first thing to come to mind.
I think of Corton & Richebourg as the two GC’s that need 25+ years. I could be wrong though…

posts.name: J Wei
posts.cooked: Another one for Bonnes-Mares. Seems to take forever to come around. . . at least in my experience. I haven’t had any BM in the 60s or 70s though.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I’d say BM as well.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Dan Kravitz:

I’m far from an expert on red Burgundy, much less Grand Crus. However IMO anything from Burgundy held over 30 years, regardless of provenance, becomes a risk and very, very subject to bottle variation.

You say that Corton comes around earlier. If your time frame is 40 - 50 years minimum (!), I guess you are right. However I am starting to find that good Cortons can open and start to sing in the 20+ range, I haven’t gotten to 30 yet.

Dan Kravitz

Agree about the Corton, Dan,

I’ve had good luck with them also in 20-5 year time frame, and also even with some Richebourg. Bonnes Mares, a few of which I own, but in fact have yet to open, makes sense as another one that takes a long time to come around. Clos St. Denis perhaps not so much?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Bonnes Mares, Grands Echezeaux, Mazis Chambertin, Corton Clos du Roi, Corton Renardes, the best renditions of Clos Vougeot.

Could be interesting to extend this discussion to 1er crus, e.g. Savigny Serpentieres…

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: for me, Corton and Bonnes Mares take the longest to mature.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: Clos Vougeot and Corton.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Kirk.Grant:

I think of Corton & Richebourg as the two GC’s that need 25+ years. I could be wrong though…

Hard to comment without discussing vintage. Are we talking about 2005 or 2007, for example.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

for me, Corton and Bonnes Mares take the longest to mature.

so much this.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked: I agree with Alan…

posts.name: J russick
posts.cooked: All though a 1st Cru, Malconsorts takes a boat load of time. Especially the Cathiard. Acts, ages and drinks like a grand cru.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
johnr:

All though a 1st Cru, Malconsorts takes a boat load of time. Especially the Cathiard. Acts, ages and drinks like a grand cru.

Is that an assessment of Sebastien’s wines or his father’s?

Keith Levenberg:

Clos Vougeot and Corton.

I wonder if there’s an odd relationship between this and wines from these two vineyards currently being more “affordable?”

I myself might add Clos de Tart. I realize the young, more recent versions aren’t fashionable around here, but they have been among the least “mature” Grand Crus I have had from other epochs in side-by-side tastings, and until the prices became too much I bought a few bottles to hide away for the very long term expecting them to show their pedigree.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Interesting how many premier crus take a tremendous time to mature. a few from Nuits would be in the frame. I also add to the list Clos de la Roche which take minimum of twenty five years for a good producer in a great vintage.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
johnr:

All though a 1st Cru, Malconsorts takes a boat load of time. Especially the Cathiard. Acts, ages and drinks like a grand cru.

the 06 has been pretty accessible for awhile now. super tasty. Same as the 02.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Corton and Musigny in my opinion.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
Mark Golodetz:

Interesting how many premier crus take a tremendous time to mature. a few from Nuits would be in the frame.

True! Vaucrains is in this bucket moreso than many (most?) grand crus. Grand crus sometimes get a wider window of drinkability from having more flesh to burn.
